name: Nut
shortDescription: Nut is the Egyptian goddess of the sky, depicted as arching over the earth, giving birth to the sun each day and swallowing it each night.
description: Nut is a central figure in Egyptian mythology, embodying the sky and heavens. She is typically depicted as a nude woman, her body arched in a semicircle over the earth, represented by her husband Geb. As the goddess of the sky, Nut plays a vital role in the cycle of day and night, as well as the journey of the sun god Ra. According to myth, Nut and Geb were initially locked in an embrace, preventing anything from existing between them. Shu, the god of air, separated them, creating space for life. Nut's daily act of giving birth to the sun and swallowing it at night symbolizes the continuous cycle of death and rebirth. She is also considered a protector of the dead, welcoming them into her star-studded body.
subtitle: Celestial Mother, Starry Vault of Heaven
achievements: Gave birth to the sun god Ra every day.
Swallowed the sun god Ra every night, ensuring his rebirth.
Protected the dead, offering them a place in the heavens.
Provided the celestial boundary between the earthly and heavenly realms.
Gave birth to Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder after winning days from Thoth in a game of Senet.
Represents the vastness of the cosmos.
quests: To give birth to and protect the sun god Ra each day.
To provide a safe passage for deceased souls into the afterlife.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Mythology
extendedContent.content: Nut's origins trace back to the earliest periods of Egyptian mythology. She is the daughter of Shu (air) and Tefnut (moisture) and the sister-wife of Geb (earth). The creation myth explains that Nut and Geb were initially inseparable, but Shu separated them to create space for the world. Angered by this separation, Ra forbade Nut from giving birth on any day of the year. However, Thoth, the god of wisdom, gambled with the moon and won five extra days, allowing Nut to give birth to Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder on these newly created days. This myth explains the origin of the Egyptian calendar and highlights Nut's role in the birth of essential deities.

extendedContent.title: Role & Significance
extendedContent.content: Nut's role as the sky goddess is fundamental to Egyptian cosmology. She is the celestial boundary that separates the chaotic waters of the heavens from the earthly realm. Her daily act of giving birth to and swallowing the sun is crucial to the Egyptian understanding of the cyclical nature of time, death, and rebirth. Nut is also a protector of the dead, believed to welcome them into her star-studded body and provide them with sustenance in the afterlife. This protective aspect made her an important figure in funerary rituals, with images of Nut often painted inside sarcophagi.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism & Interpretation
extendedContent.content: Nut's symbolism is deeply connected to the sky, heavens, and the cyclical nature of existence. Her arched body represents the dome of the sky, while the stars that adorn her represent the souls of the deceased and the celestial realm. The act of swallowing and birthing the sun symbolizes the daily cycle of death and rebirth, as well as the continuous renewal of life. Nut's association with water, both in the form of the celestial ocean and the amniotic fluid of birth, further emphasizes her role as a source of life and regeneration. In essence, Nut embodies the vastness, mystery, and life-giving power of the cosmos.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Nut's influence extends beyond ancient Egyptian religion and mythology. Her image and symbolism have inspired countless works of art, literature, and popular culture. Her role as a mother goddess and protector of the dead continues to resonate with modern audiences. Furthermore, her depiction as the arched sky has influenced architectural designs and artistic representations of the cosmos. Nut's legacy highlights the enduring power of mythology to shape our understanding of the world and inspire creativity across cultures and generations.

sources.author: R.O. Faulkner
sources.description: Translation of the Pyramid Texts, containing early references to Nut.

sources.author: Raymond O. Faulkner
sources.description: Translations of spells and texts from coffins, mentioning Nut's role in the afterlife.

associations.name: Stars
associations.significance: Adorn her body, representing souls and the celestial realm.

associations.name: Rebirth
associations.significance: Her daily swallowing and birthing of the sun.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Gaia
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Primal Earth goddess, similar to Geb, Nut's husband. Both represent the foundation of existence.

cross_cultural_parallels.name: Njord
cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: God associated with the sea and fertility, mirroring Nut's role in creating life.

companions.name: Geb
companions.description: God of the Earth, Nut's husband and brother.

companions.name: Shu
companions.description: God of the Air, Nut's father, who separated her from Geb.

companions.name: Ra
companions.description: Sun god, born of Nut each day.

companions.name: Osiris
companions.description: God of the afterlife, one of the children born to Nut after Thoth won extra days.

worshipPractices.name: Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Offerings were made to Nut to ensure safe passage into the afterlife and to honor her role as the sky goddess.

worshipPractices.name: Funerary Rites
worshipPractices.description: Images of Nut were often painted inside sarcophagi to protect the deceased and provide them with sustenance in the afterlife.

festivals.name: Birthdays of Nut's Children
festivals.timing: The five epagomenal days at the end of the Egyptian year
festivals.description: Festivals were held to celebrate the births of Osiris, Isis, Set, Nephthys, and Horus the Elder, all children of Nut.

cultural.modernLegacy: Nut's image continues to inspire artists, writers, and filmmakers. She represents the vastness of the cosmos, the cyclical nature of life, and the protective aspect of motherhood.
